Yeah, so for me, I've been a programmer for...

it was in 2006.

like they started very much like super early version of Freya's got introduced to that through them.

And that was what my attention.
So I switched from doing all UI work with Flash and Squacks to doing full stack stuff with Rails in 2006 and got pretty involved with the Ruby community for a long time.
So I did did like a lot of check.
Client training.

like 50% of my time was always training and doing events.
So I helped with like some of the RailsConf stuff, like helped with like a bunch of different states with their initiatives for like getting Ruby adoption and all that.
So pretty involved with the community in early days.
And then 2013, I wanted to like get involved with like more product, like product companies.
And product companies are not that normal, at least back then, like the startup ecosystem in Brazil was like pretty, pretty nascent.
And like BC money was not spread out super well like it's in the US.
So the product companies are not super amazing.
And all you had was like big government contracts or banks and stuff like that.
It's not fun.
So I wanted to do product stuff.
And so I sent a message to the guys.

The Seven Signals asking if they, like my plan was just like a year off and just travel around like all different countries and I work at different companies for free and like an intern or volunteer to see if I could learn anything from them.
And then eventually figure out where I wanted to be.

So I sent a message to the Seven Signals guys asking that if they, like I saw that they had just invested in the company that was doing this concept of a coding boot camp was the first one doing that.
And it was a Ruby boot camp essentially for building startups in Chicago.
like I messaged him out of the blue, didn't know him, Jason, and then Jason Fried.

They had just made an investment.
like they were co-owners of the company and the company was incubated inside the Seven Signals.

So I ended up working for this company for three years.
It was incubated inside 2017, so I was flying back and forth between Brazil and Chicago for a while.

And then we kind of missed the boat, and we kind of made some mistakes where other boot camps became bigger, and the technical side of our product didn't go in the same direction as Codecademy, for example.
Codecademy became like a SaaS project.

missed the boat, the opportunity, when the opportunity closed there, so I decided to move to the Bay Area, and I joined a company called If This Then That, and that was 2015, something around 2014 or 15.

We managed to, like, separate things really well.
The product team faster to build.

had a different mindset of how the team should be structured, how the product team and the engineering team should be built, and all that, and the kind of processes that we wanted to implement.

And so they have this big corner of a product here with a bunch of little products around, all making good revenue, and they wanted to turn those into separate companies.
And I took one that was like a younger product, was just one person that was running it, had already spun off from BaseAmp a year before.
So I joined that as a technical co-founder.
We didn't do that for the last seven years before joining GrowthX.

So that we had a kind of a flat six months that I didn't like.
And at the same time, we were also having the inflection point of GPT.

GPT GPT 3.5, and I was already toying with it, was already trying to build some features with it, and my girlfriend is a machine learning engineer, and she was working with language models before GPT came out with BERT for performance circle space, for patient data, like she was paying a ton of attention, and we were talking about this at home all the time, and I was toying with some features for Canopy as well, trying to build like a chatbot for training content.
And when 3.5 came out, was just, okay, this really works.
Like, I had, I built the reg system, had all the chunking, and the semantic chunking, like adding the metadata on the chunks and all that, and like, then it started to really work.

And, but it was always this feature bolted, bolted, bolted into another project that was meant for a bunch of other things, and like, so that's when I was like, okay, like actually, what, what are the things that we can do today that were not possible before?
it's pretty.
Predicting that the models would keep getting better, so that's when I decided to take a break and just spend six months studying as much as possible.

And he had spent a ton of time figuring out, before AI, figuring out how to build publication teams.

So he did that, that's AI, HashiCorp, IBM.
And some other places, and it always worked out, and now he could do that with a much smaller team, and he started to just try to do that, teaching people how to do it.

he started doing, like, essentially, AIOps workshops, and people were excited, they didn't have the, like, AIOps is not an easy thing to learn, they didn't have the infrastructure to do it themselves, we started doing that for them, and it would build an agency for, creating publications inside of bigger companies, essentially.

And so that's, when I joined him, so, like, we had this workspace inside of AIOps with, like, 20 clients, and, like, dozens of workflows for each client, and, like, every new client would duplicate the workflows, and, like, the maintenance and all, that was pretty hairy.
So we ended up, like, moving out of AIOps into, like, a code-based LLM orchestration project that we created.
And like...
So we built a framework on top of Temporal, where we can create our own workflows and generate the workflows in plain text and things like that.
And essentially just trying to be able to generate workflows faster for ourselves and maintain them in code-based style, conversioning and everything.
And so that's essentially what we did from September to January.
And then things grew really fast, so we ended up fundraising to try to build some of the other things we needed.
Because we needed not just the runtime of the workflows, but we needed also the system for the process for people to follow the guardrails.
So they're not bumping into things like Airtable and Slack and Notion and all that.
And they're having to attend meeting with clients just to do some editorial stuff, like review fact checks and stuff here and there.
So like we ended up building all the, we were still doing that, building all the infrastructure.
For the agency team to scale, and we're also building, we're continuing to build on the framework that we did for our workflows and all that.
we're adding support for other things like agents and MCP servers and all that.
So building a little IDE on top of that as well.

I mean, you not only can receive steps, you can receive modifications of steps.
Say a user, like for example, Google Feed will send you that the person did 200 steps as an optimistic calculation, but then we'll recalculate until, you know, actually that was 150.
And being able to like subtract, extract and doing that.
It was a bit of a hassle.
I wanted to build a few on SQS, and then we ran the calculation on lambdas, but then you didn't have many different kinds of challenges.

So we will separate the calculation in different queues based on the challenge and also based on the people that were involved in the challenge so that we can have different rules of queuing and be able to run a different bunch of stuff in parallel whenever we could.
That was a challenge.
That was crazy.
